item.title: Stevie Wonder
item.description: Genres: Soul, Pop soul, funk, art pop Scene: Detroit / Motown Records Era: 1961–present (primary period: 1971–1976) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The riff that opens “Superstition” — that dirty, stacked Hohner Clavinet line — Stevie Wonder built alone, laying down the drums first and then overdubbing ...

item.title: The Beach Boys
item.description: Genres: Surf rock, vocal surf, baroque pop, sunshine pop, psychedelic pop, progressive pop, pop rock Scene: Hawthorne / Hollywood, California Era: 1961–present (primary period: 1963–1970) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The Beach Boys are one instrument made of five throats.

item.title: The Supremes
item.description: Genres: Soul, Pop soul, Girl group Scene: Detroit / Motown Records Era: 1959–1977 (classic lineup 1961–1967) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify On April 8, 1964, Diana Ross sang “Where Did Our Love Go” the way she wanted to, high and bright, and Holland-Dozier-Holland made her do it again, lower, in a brea...

item.title: The Beatles
item.description: Genres: Pop rock, psychedelic pop, art rock Scene: Merseybeat Movement: British beat boom Era: British Invasion (1963–1970) Playlist: FAB RYM · Apple Music · Spotify One chord opens A Hard Day’s Night, and no two musicians have ever agreed how to name it.

item.title: Laura Nyro
item.description: Genres: Pop soul, singer-songwriter, progressive soul Scene: Brill Building (adjacent), New York City Era: 1966–1997 (primary period: 1967–1971) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The piano on “Timer” (1968) shifts from a ballad’s tenderness to a percussive, almost violent intensity in the space of a breat...

item.title: The Temptations
item.description: Genres: Soul, Pop soul, psychedelic soul Scene: Detroit / Motown Records Era: 1960–present (classic era 1964–1971) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The Motown act that best illustrates the label’s capacity for reinvention.

item.title: Lott — Love and Theft
item.description: Author: Eric Lott Title: Love and Theft: Blackface Minstrelsy and the American Working Class Year: 1993 Type: Academic book (Oxford University Press) A study of blackface minstrelsy that reframes white America’s relationship with Black culture as simultaneously one of desire and domination.

item.title: Miller — Segregating Sound
item.description: Author: Karl Hagstrom Miller Title: Segregating Sound: Inventing Folk and Pop Music in the Age of Jim Crow Year: 2010 Type: Academic book (Duke University Press) A revisionist history of American music’s racial categories that argues the distinction between “Black music” and “white music” was an ind...

item.title: Warwick — Girl Groups, Girl Culture
item.description: Author: Jacqueline Warwick Title: Girl Groups, Girl Culture: Popular Music and Identity in the 1960s Year: 2007 Type: Academic book (Routledge Studies in Popular Music) Warwick takes the records the rock canon filed under “disposable teen pop” (the Brill Building product, written by men, run by men)...

item.title: Marvin Gaye
item.description: Genres: Soul, Pop soul, psychedelic soul, quiet storm Scene: Detroit / Motown Records Era: 1961–1984 (primary period: 1964–1973) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify Listen to the shift between “I Heard It Through the Grapevine” (1968) and What’s Going On (1971): from a single built on paranoia and rhythmic ...
